summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief post linking to Mencius Moldbug's analysis of why Wolfram Alpha fails as a product. The core argument, conveyed entirely through the quoted passage, is that users cannot form accurate mental models of what Wolfram Alpha can and cannot do. Because the system is built on a million lines of constantly changing code, users' attempts to predict its capabilities are inherently inadequate and error-prone. Joel lets the quote speak for itself without additional commentary.
key_points: Users fail with Wolfram Alpha because they cannot build accurate mental models of what the system understands
The gap between a complex system's actual capabilities and users' expectations creates a fundamentally broken user experience
A system that requires users to guess what it can do — and constantly re-learn as it changes — imposes an unreasonable cognitive burden
money_quotes: They create an incomplete model of the giant electronic brain in their own, non-giant, non-electronic brains.
Since the giant electronic brain is a million lines of code which is constantly changing, this is a painful, inadequate and error-prone task.
key_insight: A product fails when users cannot form a reliable mental model of what it can do, forcing them to guess at its capabilities through painful trial and error.
